quality.rationale: Links Lorentz group algebra to topology via celestial sphere mapping; requires multi-step conceptual bridging; solution sketches proof rigorously but omits topological degree details.

origin.question: Problem 1.27. Show that any proper (non time-reversing, non parity-reversing) homogeneous Lorentz transformation leaves fixed at least one null direction.## SolutionSolution 1.27. An astronaut looking at the sky (by null photons!) makes an arbitrary Lorentz transformation, and looks at the sky again. There results a continuous map of the sky onto itself. Any continuous map of a 2-sphere onto itself has at least one fixed point.
origin.answer:
Any proper homogeneous Lorentz transformation possesses at least one invariant null direction ($v^\mu 
eq 0$ with $g_{\mu
u}v^\mu v^
u = 0$ and $\Lambda^\mu{}_
u v^
u = v^\mu$).
origin.solution: An astronaut looking at the sky (by null photons!) makes an arbitrary Lorentz transformation, and looks at the sky again. There results a continuous map of the sky onto itself. Any continuous map of a 2-sphere onto itself has at least one fixed point.
